title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] KIRUPA CHINNATHAMBI:
Hi, everyone. Today, we're going to learn
about how Firebase Studio can help you build high-quality
full-stack AI apps quickly. My name is Kirupa Chinnathambi,
and I'm a PM on Firebase Studio. I'm joined by my
colleague Rody Davis, who is a developer relations
engineer also on the same team. We're going to start by getting
a 30,000-foot overview of how app development has changed and
why we built Firebase Studio. We will follow up with a deeper
look at Firebase Studio's marquee features, along with a
live demo that really highlights the breadth and depth
of capabilities that can help you be productive. So there are three big
changes to app development over the years that
played a big role in us building Firebase Studio. First, app development has
gotten really difficult. Building apps today is time
consuming, often frustrating, and filled with numerous
hoops we need to jump through. And this complexity spans
the full software development lifecycle. All stops on this path
are more complicated now than they've ever been. This complexity is
particularly frustrating if you're a
professional developer. You and I don't have the luxury
of skipping steps or cutting corners. Some of the complexities
you need to wrangle include answering what your
technology stack should be. What are your dependencies? Is what you're building secure,
scalable, and performant? Depending on your project,
your team, and the industry you're working in, the types
of questions you need to answer can be numerous and varied. All of this can be
hugely overwhelming. The software
development lifecycle should be smoother and
easier for all of us to navigate as we
build our apps. A trend that we've been
observing for some time, that really accelerated in
the last few years, is that software
development is becoming more decentralized and
collaborative across devices, geographies, and time zones. It used to be that all we
needed to get our apps built was our primary, often
powerful, computer.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
is that software
development is becoming more decentralized and
collaborative across devices, geographies, and time zones. It used to be that all we
needed to get our apps built was our primary, often
powerful, computer. Now, over time, we
started to take our work onto our personal devices. These could be our
laptops, phones, tablets, and an even smaller
devices like watches. Beyond just building our apps
across a variety of devices, we also now work from
a variety of locations. It could be a coffee shop. It could be a
secondary workplace. It could even be our homes. To add more color
to the situation, we now collaborate closely
with other people, each of whom could be working
on the same project from different locations,
different devices, and maybe even different time zones. Third, and one that is probably
very top of mind for all of us, is the role AI assistants
and powerful LLMs are playing in
completely changing how we as developers build apps. This visualization really drives
home the two extreme workflows you, me, and our
teams are likely adopting when building apps. On one end, we
have a world where we are in the driver's
seat, and the AI plays a purely assistive role. On the other end,
we have a world where the AI is doing almost
all of the heavy lifting. We're playing more
of a consulting role and nudging the AI into the
right direction if needed. The complexity of
problems AI can solve, though, has grown
by leaps and bounds. Today, a quarter of all new code
at Google is generated by AI. Beyond just
productivity, use of AI also tends to increase overall
satisfaction and happiness. Here's where a study that showed
individuals assigned to use AI were both productive
and more happy than individuals and teams
who didn't use AI at all. This chart highlights
what I said a second ago, where the use of AI
pretty much leads to an increase in
positive emotions and a decrease in
negative emotions. This is pretty powerful. In recognizing these three major
shifts, a handful of us who
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
This chart highlights
what I said a second ago, where the use of AI
pretty much leads to an increase in
positive emotions and a decrease in
negative emotions. This is pretty powerful. In recognizing these three major
shifts, a handful of us who love developer tools got
together a few years ago and asked, can we do better? We landed on a
handful of principles. First, the ideal environment
needs to be ubiquitous. You should be able to build
any app from any device, from anywhere. You and I should
have easy access to integrated solutions,
not individual puzzle pieces that we have to figure out how
to combine in the right way. AI assistants shouldn't just
be limited to coding scenarios or tacked on as an afterthought. AI assistance needs to
be thoughtfully woven throughout the entirety of the
software development lifecycle. Now, software
development has always been a collaborative activity. And we need to ensure
that our tools help here, where we can be working across
a variety of devices, locations, and time zones. To help answer the question
of "Can we do better?," we're thrilled to
announce Firebase Studio. Firebase Studio is an integrated
and extensible agentic workspace to build, run, and
manage web apps, cross-platform mobile apps,
backend services, and more. Let's walk through some
of the high-level features that Firebase Studio
brings to the table. First, it is fully web-based. All you need is a browser
and an internet connection. And just because
it's in a browser, though, does not mean you have
to sacrifice capabilities. You have a full
Cloud VM with access to the file system, terminal,
and other necessary commands. It's also built on the
familiar VS Code environment. You have easy starting
points, configurable using a technology called NixOS. AI assistance and
collaborative features are integrated throughout. Now, Firebase Studio may look
very familiar to many of you. It is built on the learnings
we received from the hundreds and thousands of you
who use Project IDX and gave us your feedback.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
are integrated throughout. Now, Firebase Studio may look
very familiar to many of you. It is built on the learnings
we received from the hundreds and thousands of you
who use Project IDX and gave us your feedback. We actually went public
with Project IDX at I/O just last year. So it's great to highlight
the evolution of IDX into Firebase Studio with
you all at this year's I/O conference. Now, let's take a look
at Firebase Studio and walk through its
most common features. When you launch Firebase
Studio for the first time, you'll have a bunch
of options on how you'd like to get started. You can start with our
app prototyper, where you specify what
kind of app you'd like to build using multimodal,
natural-language prompts. In this mode, you can
ask us to build an app. Once you do that,
we first translate your request into something
we call a blueprint. Think of a blueprint as a
one-pager or a mini PRD. When you're OK with
the requirements, we start building your app. You can continue to interact
with the AI assistants to fine tune the app's output. You can draw directly
onto the preview surface with our annotation feature. You can select elements
and describe the changes you would like to see. Once you are ready to share
your application with the world, you can choose to deploy
to Firebase App Hosting or make your app temporarily
available with our preview mode. Now, going back to the
Firebase Studio landing page, you can also create
a new workspace and build it
entirely from scratch or choose from a
collection of templates. We provide some of the
most common templates available to you for
easy, direct access. You can explore
our full collection of templates, which span
the most common starting points for frontend,
backend, mobile, and more. If you want to go one level
deeper, below the templates, we give you a lot of
flexibility to specify how your environment is
built all the way down to the VM-specific details,
build tools, and more. You can also bring your existing
project into Firebase Studio.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
we give you a lot of
flexibility to specify how your environment is
built all the way down to the VM-specific details,
build tools, and more. You can also bring your existing
project into Firebase Studio. You can choose to open your
project from popular Git providers like GitHub,
Bitbucket, and GitLab, or you can upload a zip file
of your existing project from your local computer. Independent of how
you get started with building your app
in Firebase Studio, we give you full
agentic capabilities throughout with
our AI assistants. Changes you ask for can be
made to a single line of code or to an entire code base. This is the power of Gemini
exposed through our app development environment. Beyond our built-in AI systems,
agents from Gemini Code Assist will also be fully available. Our goal is to ensure
you have the flexibility to choose how much AI
assistance you want. You can use a lot of AI,
or you can use some AI. You're fully in control. Lastly, we talked about the
importance of collaboration when it comes to building apps. Firebase Studio also share
your entire workspace with your colleagues and
build apps in real time just as naturally as you might
if you're working physically side by side with someone. Before I hand it
over to Rody, who will walk through
some live demos, a main takeaway is that Firebase
Studio is a professional tool designed for all developers. Whether you're AI first, code
first, frontend, backend, greenfield, or brownfield,
we have you covered. All are welcome. Now let's turn it over to Rody. RODY DAVIS: Hi, my name is Rody. So now it's time to see
Firebase Studio in action. There are two main
modes that you can start from inside of Firebase Studio. The first is an IDE view,
which you should already be familiar with because
it's the same IDE from IDX. And the second is a new
app prototyping agent. With this new app
prototyping agent, you can build
full-stack applications from natural language. It will start by creating
a blueprint for us to review, which we
can modify at will,

title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
And the second is a new
app prototyping agent. With this new app
prototyping agent, you can build
full-stack applications from natural language. It will start by creating
a blueprint for us to review, which we
can modify at will, and then continue to iterate
on in the app with the chat. When you're happy
with the overview, you can start the
app generation, and the blueprint will be
saved inside the project alongside the code. The fully featured
application it creates is backed by Next.js, connected
to Genkit and Firebase. If you're building an AI
feature that needs an API key, we will create a
Firebase project for you in addition to
generating the API key. As you request new features
or modify existing ones, Gemini will update
Genkit flows and wire up any of the multimodal requests
and handling inside of React for you. When you feel like the app
is in a good place to share, we have an easy
one-click Publish button that will take the
Next.js application and deploy it with
Firebase App Hosting. As you make changes
to your code, you can also create
new rollouts by going through the exact same flow. We also support other
deployment options, too, which you can take advantage
of in the code editor. Firebase Studio has a powerful
integrated development environment which lets
you bring existing code bases with any stack configured
by the Nix ecosystem. And there's also many
templates to get started with, with common frameworks and
languages that you use. For those that aren't
aware, Nix is probably one of the most supported
package ecosystems in the Linux world. There's over 120,000
packages to choose from. Every workspace
in Firebase Studio is running on a full
VM powered by NixOS. This means that you can run
most commands in the terminal that you would be
able to locally. This could be anything from
setting up and configuring databases, to
running Docker files, or even running both Python node
and go all in the same terminal at the same time. Just like how the app
prototyper has a powerful chat,

title: Google I/O 2025 – x2zvki_VlRE
text:
This could be anything from
setting up and configuring databases, to
running Docker files, or even running both Python node
and go all in the same terminal at the same time. Just like how the app
prototyper has a powerful chat, we also have the full
capabilities of Gemini right inside of our IDE. Gemini can read and write
files and run terminal commands with the context
of your project. You can also choose
a custom model with an API key, upload
attachments or drawings, and have multiple chat
histories persisted for later. There are so many more things
we did not cover, but make sure to check out our Firebase
YouTube channel for more videos and our blog for any updates. Head over to Firebase
Studio to learn more and try it out for yourself. We are in preview now,
and we would love feedback on the product for things
that are going great and things that can be improved. Thanks for watching,
and we can't wait to see what you'll
build with Firebase Studio. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
